name: Six Banded Angelfish
diet: Algae, sponges and tunicates
behavior: Semi-aggressive. Juveniles inhabit shallow inner reefs and are secretive. Main predators include sharks and larger predatory fish.
reproduction: Broadcast spawners — release sperm and eggs at the surface, fertilized eggs get carried with the current until they settle on the seafloor.
funFact: When alarmed, these fish make a grunting sound to warn others nearby.

name: Blue Ringed Angelfish
diet: Zooplankton, algae, sponges and tunicates
behavior: Live in a harem — one male to several females. Adults often found in pairs. All juveniles are born female.
reproduction: All juveniles are born female. Undergo complete colour change when transitioning from juvenile to adult.
funFact: All juveniles are born female and undergo a complete colour change when changing from juvenile to adult.

name: Beaked Coralfish
diet: Benthic invertebrates — invertebrates that live on the seafloor
behavior: Territorial. Breeding pairs are monogamous. Once they find their partner, they will stay together for life.
reproduction: Oviparous — lay eggs with little embryonic development within mother.
funFact: Once they find their partner, they stay together for life — one of the most loyal fish on the reef.

name: Eight Banded Butterflyfish
diet: Feeds exclusively on coral polyps
behavior: Breeding pairs are monogamous. Juveniles are found in groups among Acropora coral.
reproduction: Dioecism reproduction — egg scatterers.
funFact: Feeds exclusively on coral polyps, making it a strong indicator of coral reef health.

name: Orange Banded Coralfish
diet: Omnivorous — sponges, benthic algae and small invertebrates
behavior: Form pairs during breeding. Live in coral reefs, in rocky or rubbly areas with poor to good coral growth.
reproduction: Oviparous — lay eggs with little embryonic development within mother. Form pairs during breeding.
funFact: Unlike many butterflyfish, they thrive in areas with poor coral growth — not just pristine reefs.

name: Lined Butterflyfish
diet: Coral polyps, small anemones, algae and invertebrates
behavior: Highly territorial — linked to eating habits in particular areas. Benthopelagic. Does not rely on live coral for food.
reproduction: Thought to spawn near their habitats during the time of the new moon.
funFact: Thought to spawn during the new moon — one of few reef fish with a lunar breeding cycle.

name: Weibel's Butterflyfish
diet: Coral polyps, algae and invertebrates
behavior: Main feeding technique is grazing algae off rocks. Found on shallow rocky coral reefs. Monogamous.
reproduction: Oviparous species who breed in pairs. Monogamous.
funFact: Rather than picking at coral like most butterflyfish, they primarily graze algae off rocks.

name: Harlequin Sweetlips
diet: Benthic invertebrates, and sometimes other fish — nocturnal hunters
behavior: Adults are solitary, live in sheltering ledges or caves. Nocturnal hunters.
funFact: Juveniles are Batesian mimics of poisonous flatworms — copying their colour and swimming style to avoid predators.

name: Longfin Bannerfish
diet: Zooplankton, coral polyps and sometimes benthic invertebrates
behavior: Juveniles feed by cleaning other fish. Uses tactile (touch) and chemical communication as well as sound by muscle movements for courtship and territory.
reproduction: Pelagic spawners — eggs hatch 18–30 hours after release in waters of 29°C. Form pairs during breeding.
funFact: Juveniles work as cleaner fish, picking parasites off larger fish — switching careers to coral feeding as adults.

name: Hexagon Grouper
diet: Small fish and crustaceans (including mantis shrimp) — hunts near the bottom at dusk
behavior: Well camouflaged. Solitary. Food is drawn into their mouths by rapid pressure change, swallowing prey whole.
reproduction: Protogynous hermaphrodites — change sex from female to male. Females change to males above 20cm in length.
funFact: Changes sex from female to male at some point in their life — females transform once they exceed 20cm in length.

name: Blacktip Grouper
diet: Smaller fish and crustaceans (sometimes octopus) — ambush predators
behavior: Ambush predator. Food is drawn into their mouths by powerful suction — they swallow prey whole.
reproduction: Simultaneous and sequential hermaphrodites. The largest in the group often loses female function and reproduces exclusively as a male.
funFact: Can function as both male and female simultaneously — the largest individual often loses female function entirely.

name: Blue-lined Grouper
diet: Small fish and crustaceans
behavior: Solitary species. Found in shallow waters over silty or dead reefs.
funFact: Unlike most groupers that prefer pristine coral, this species thrives on silty, dead, or degraded reefs.

name: Slingjaw Wrasse
diet: Small crustaceans living in the coral and small fish
behavior: Generally solitary. Protogynous hermaphrodites. Colours of males are more intense during courtship. Sometimes visits Cleaner Wrasse for cleaning.
reproduction: Protogynous hermaphrodites — change sex from female to male.
funFact: Has a protractable jaw that shoots out to form a tube half the length of its body — one of the most extreme jaw extensions of any fish.

name: Red Breasted Wrasse
diet: Mainly benthic, hard-shelled invertebrates (including sea urchins)
behavior: Usually inhabit areas with a mixture of sand, coral and rubble. Often solitary. Distinct pairing during breeding.
reproduction: Oviparous. Distinct pairing during breeding.
funFact: Specialises in eating hard-shelled invertebrates — including sea urchins that most other fish avoid.

name: Humphead Wrasse
diet: Molluscs and a wide variety of well-armoured invertebrates — even toxic prey such as Crown-of-Thorns starfish, boxfishes or sea hares
behavior: Among the largest reef fishes, weighing up to 191kg. Usually solitary. Has a home cave where it sleeps. Juveniles occur among branching corals in shallow lagoons.
funFact: One of the few fish that eats Crown-of-Thorns starfish — a major coral predator — making it a key protector of reef health.

name: Moon Wrasse
diet: Mainly benthic invertebrates and fish eggs
behavior: Territorial, chasing other fish away. Protogynous hermaphrodites — the process takes 10 days.
reproduction: Unique mating ritual where the male changes colour and chases the female until she releases her eggs. The male then fertilises and protects them until they hatch.
funFact: The male changes colour during courtship and chases the female until she releases her eggs — then guards them until hatching.

name: Bluestreak Cleaner Wrasse
diet: Crustacean ectoparasites and mucus of other fish
behavior: Most common cleaner wrasse on reefs. Stays in cleaning stations where other fish come to be cleaned. Monogamous. Communicates through body postures and movements.
reproduction: Protogynous hermaphrodites. Pelagic spawners. Sex reversal takes 14–18 days.
funFact: Mimicked by the fin-nipping blenny Aspidontus taeniatus, which pretends to be a cleaner but bites chunks from unsuspecting fish.

name: Two-Banded Soapfish
diet: Mainly fishes — surprises relatively large prey using its expandable jaws
behavior: Found near caves and crevices in rocky and coral reefs.
funFact: When stressed, it excretes a skin toxin called grammistin — making it taste like soap, hence the name.

name: Two-lined Monocle Bream
diet: Benthic invertebrates and small fishes
behavior: Common on coral reefs. Often appear singularly. Uses a lateral line organ to detect prey under the sand. Protogynous hermaphrodite.
reproduction: Protogynous hermaphrodite — changes from female to male.
funFact: Juveniles mimic poison-fanged blennies to avoid predators — a disguise they drop once they grow larger.

name: Long Snout Clingfish
diet: Burrowing bivalves, tube feet of sea urchins and shrimp eggs
behavior: Live in close association with long-spined sea urchins and branching coral.
funFact: Males and females have different snout lengths — females have longer snouts adapted for eating shrimp eggs, males eat urchin tube feet. This is called sexual dimorphism.

name: Giant Grouper
diet: Opportunistic — fish, crustaceans, small sharks, juvenile turtles
behavior: Largest bony fish found around coral reefs. Benthopelagic and benthic. Solitary and long-lived. Often occurs in a home cave. Vulnerable to fishing.
reproduction: Diandric protogynous hermaphrodites. Can take up to 25 years to reach sexual maturity. Spawn on a lunar cycle (about 7 days). Aggregative broadcast spawners.
funFact: Can take up to 25 years to reach sexual maturity and spawns on a lunar cycle — making it extremely vulnerable to overfishing.

name: Malabar Grouper
diet: Primarily fishes, crustaceans and sometimes cephalopods
behavior: Found in coral and rocky reefs. Common length around 1m.
reproduction: Protogynous hermaphrodites — change sex from female to male.
funFact: Can reach a massive 2.3 metres in length — one of the largest grouper species on Indo-Pacific reefs.

name: Marbled Grouper
diet: Mainly crustaceans, fishes, cephalopods and gastropods
behavior: Often found in coral-rich lagoons and outer reefs. Usually found in small schools but sometimes solitary.
funFact: One of the few grouper species regularly found in small schools rather than being strictly solitary.

name: Orange Spotted Grouper
diet: Fishes, crabs, shrimps and other benthic invertebrates
behavior: Females reach maturity at 25cm (2–3 years). Eggs and larvae are likely to be pelagic.
reproduction: Protogynous hermaphrodites — transition from female to male occurs at 55–75cm.
funFact: Transitions from female to male between 55–75cm — a dramatic life change triggered by size and social cues.

name: Bluespotted Ribbontail Ray
diet: Molluscs, worms, shrimp and crabs
behavior: Bottom dwelling — seeks shelter under coral and rock in the day. Nocturnal hunter. Venomous. Males become sexually mature when the disc reaches 20cm.
reproduction: Ovoviviparous — live young in a sack, where they eat the yolk. Distinct pairing when breeding. Can give birth to up to 7 young.
funFact: Gives birth to live young that develop inside egg sacs — can have up to 7 pups in a single litter.

name: Spotted Eagle Ray
diet: Polychaetes, bivalves, gastropods, cephalopods, shrimps and small fish
behavior: Swims close to the surface, occasionally jumping out of the water. Usually solitary but sometimes found in groups. Hunted by sharks.
reproduction: Ovoviviparous. Bears litters of up to 4. Copulation lasts from 20 seconds to 1 minute.
funFact: Occasionally leaps completely out of the water — scientists still debate whether this is to remove parasites, escape predators, or just for fun.

name: Jenkins' Whipray
diet: Mainly small bony fish and crustaceans
behavior: Found in coastal and brackish waters, amongst sandy and silty substrates.
reproduction: Ovoviviparous. Males reach sexual maturity at 75–85cm.
funFact: Can tolerate brackish (mixed salt and fresh) water — one of the more adaptable ray species.

name: Banded Sea Krait
diet: Eels and small fish — swallows prey whole
behavior: Venomous — paralyses prey. Semiaquatic. Females significantly larger than males. Juveniles stay closer to the coast. Adults split time equally between land and sea.
reproduction: Oviparous. Can have egg clusters of up to 10. Copulation typically lasts 2 hours.
funFact: Their paddle-like tail doubles as a decoy — predators mistake it for the head, giving the krait time to escape.

name: Crocodilefish
diet: Small fish and crustaceans
behavior: Lives in sand or rubble substrates. Camouflaged. Ambush predator.
funFact: Masters of camouflage — they lie perfectly still on sandy bottoms, nearly invisible until you're right on top of them.

name: Flasher Scorpionfish
diet: Fishes
behavior: Nocturnal ambush predator. Venomous glands on spine.
reproduction: Pelagic spawners — can release up to 1,500 eggs at a time.
predators: Sharks, rays, large snappers and groupers
funFact: Named for the bright colours hidden under its pectoral fins — it 'flashes' them to startle predators or attract mates.

name: Spiny Devilfish
diet: Small fishes and crustaceans
behavior: Benthic fish found on sandy and coral reefs. Buries itself. Uses pectoral fins to startle predators and show colours during courtship.
funFact: Venomous enough to be deadly to humans — the recommended treatment is immersion in hot water to break down the toxin.

name: Lionfish
diet: Small fishes, shrimp and crabs — ambush predators
behavior: Often solitary. Blows jets of water to confuse prey and uses pectoral fins to block their escape. Dorsal fins are venomous. Aposematic — warning colouration tells predators they are not worth eating.
predators: Sharks, eels, larger snappers and groupers
funFact: Blows jets of water to confuse prey, then uses its fan-like pectoral fins to corner them — a surprisingly strategic hunter.

name: Pinktail Triggerfish
diet: Mainly algae and detritus (dead organic matter), as well as crustaceans, octopus and sponges
behavior: Common in areas over-exposed to currents. Benthopelagic. Distinct mating pairs.
reproduction: Oviparous. Distinct mating pairs.
funFact: Thrives in strong currents that most reef fish avoid — you'll often find them where the water moves fastest.

name: Yellow Margin Triggerfish
diet: Coral branches, gastropods, crustaceans, tunicates and sea urchins
behavior: Generally solitary or in pairs. Exhibit biparental care — only females tend the eggs but both parents keep guard.
reproduction: Oviparous. Build nests 2m wide and 0.5m deep in spawning grounds. Males arrive first, females follow days later. Can lay up to 430,000 eggs at a time, then cover them in rubble.
funFact: Builds enormous nests — 2 metres wide and half a metre deep — and can lay up to 430,000 eggs in a single spawning event.

name: Titan Triggerfish
diet: Sea urchins, coral, crabs, crustaceans, Crown-of-Thorns starfish and molluscs
behavior: Largest triggerfish species. Hostile when caring for eggs. Seen solitary or in pairs. Strong teeth adapted to their diet. Trigger fin comes up when threatened.
reproduction: Oviparous. Females are commonly the larger of the mating pairs.
funFact: Notoriously aggressive when guarding eggs — their territory extends in a cone shape upward from the nest, so swim horizontally away, not up!

name: Saddleback Anemonefish
diet: Omnivorous — zooplankton, benthic crustaceans and algae
behavior: Monogamous. Aggressively territorial, defending its host anemone against butterflyfish and other fish. Dominance hierarchy — female is the largest.
reproduction: Sequential hermaphrodite — if the female dies, the breeding male changes sex. Oviparous, demersal spawning. Males guard and aerate the eggs.
funFact: If the female dies, the breeding male changes sex to female, and the largest non-breeding male steps up as the new breeding male.

name: Pink Anemonefish
diet: Omnivorous — zooplankton, algae, small invertebrates
behavior: Monogamous. Dominance hierarchy — female is the largest. Males guard and aerate eggs.
reproduction: Oviparous. Demersal spawning. Protogynous hermaphrodite at 5.4cm. Annual fecundity rate of 2,000–4,000 eggs.
funFact: Despite their small size, a breeding pair can produce 2,000–4,000 eggs per year — prolific little parents.

name: Stellate Pufferfish
diet: Benthic invertebrates, sponges, algae and coral polyps
behavior: Mainly solitary. Territorial. No pelvic fin. Body covered in prickles. Highly poisonous — tetrodotoxin.
reproduction: Oviparous. Pelagic spawners.
funFact: At 120cm, it's one of the largest pufferfish species — and contains enough tetrodotoxin to be lethal if consumed.

name: Blotched Porcupinefish
diet: Sea urchins, crustaceans and gastropods
behavior: Hides in caves and under ledges in the day, forages at night. Solitary. In defence, can swallow water to inflate themselves.
funFact: Doubly defended — contains lethal tetrodotoxin AND can inflate itself into a spiny ball that's almost impossible to swallow.

name: Blackspotted Puffer
diet: Corals, crustaceans, molluscs, sponges, tunicates and algae
behavior: Sometimes solitary, adults often found in pairs. Territorial. Highly poisonous — tetrodotoxin. Can inflate as self-defence.
reproduction: Oviparous.
funFact: One of the few pufferfish commonly found in pairs — most species are strictly solitary.

name: Blackspotted Porcupinefish
diet: Hard-shelled creatures — urchins, gastropods and hermit crabs
behavior: Commonly seen in caves and under ledges. Solitary. Nocturnal. Adults are benthic, juveniles up to 20cm are pelagic.
funFact: Juveniles live a completely different lifestyle — drifting in open water as pelagic fish until they reach 20cm, then settling on the reef.

name: Yellow Boxfish
diet: Algae, invertebrates, crustaceans, molluscs, sponges
behavior: Juveniles live in Acropora coral. Benthopelagic. Solitary. Mates in harem — one male to 2–4 females. As they mature, they lose their vibrant colour and spots.
reproduction: Mates in harem. When stressed releases tetrodotoxin, which blocks fast voltage-gated sodium channels — leading to paralysis of voluntary muscles.
funFact: The Mercedes-Benz Bionic concept car was inspired by the yellow boxfish to demonstrate aerodynamics and stability.

name: Parrotfish
diet: Algae scraped off coral and rocks, plus detritus
behavior: Often solitary but sometimes in small groups. Mostly uses pectoral fins to swim. Excretes sand.
reproduction: Protogynous hermaphrodites. Sex change occurs at 29cm, 7 years old. Oviparous. Distinct breeding pairs.
funFact: Produces a mucus sleeping bag at night — either to mask its scent from predators, or as an alarm system that bursts and wakes it if a predator gets too close.

name: Stick Pipefish
diet: Zooplankton and benthic crustaceans
behavior: Found on sandy bottoms and on reefs.
reproduction: Ovoviviparous. Males carry eggs in a brood pouch found underneath their tail.
funFact: Like seahorses, it's the males who get pregnant — carrying eggs in a special brood pouch under their tail.

name: Brown Banded Pipefish
diet: Zooplankton and benthic crustaceans
behavior: Inhabit coral reefs. Monogamous. Males may be brooding at just 5–5.5cm.
reproduction: Ovoviviparous. Males carry eggs in a brood pouch. Males may be brooding at 5–5.5cm. Monogamous.
funFact: Males can become fathers at just 5cm long — one of the smallest brooding fish on the reef.

name: Janss' Pipefish
diet: Parasites and small crustaceans from other fish
behavior: Found in sheltered coral reefs and caves. Active cleaners — work in pairs at cleaning stations for damselfish and cardinalfish.
reproduction: Ovoviviparous. Males carry eggs in a brood pouch.
funFact: One of the few pipefish that works as a cleaner — running cleaning stations in pairs where other fish queue up for parasite removal.

name: White Eyed Moray Eel
diet: Small fish, crustaceans and octopi
behavior: Simultaneous hermaphrodites. Resilient. Has vomerine teeth that help hold prey. Known to cooperate with groupers when hunting.
reproduction: Simultaneous hermaphrodites — can function as both sexes at once.
funFact: Hunts cooperatively with groupers — the moray flushes prey from crevices while the grouper waits in open water to catch escaping fish.

name: Undulate Moray Eel
diet: Fishes, octopi and crustaceans
behavior: Benthic. Cavernicolous (cave-dwelling). Nocturnal hunter. Has vomerine teeth that help hold prey.
funFact: A true cave-dweller (cavernicolous) — spends its days hidden deep in reef crevices and only emerges to hunt at night.

name: Hawksbill Turtle
diet: Predominantly sponges, but also jellyfish
behavior: One of the smallest sea turtle species. Forage on coral reefs. Hunted for shells, eggs and meat. Reach maturity at 20 years old.
reproduction: Coastal development and beach erosion has led to the loss of nesting sites.
predators: Humans (hunted for shells, eggs and meat), sharks, large fish
funFact: The first reptile ever recorded to be biofluorescent — they glow under certain light conditions, a discovery made only in 2015.

name: Green Turtle
diet: Red, brown and green algae on coral reefs
behavior: Found in tropical and sub-tropical seas worldwide. Cannot retract neck into body. Typically swim at 2.5–3 km/hour. Has symbiotic relationship with coral reef fish at cleaning stations.
reproduction: Nest temperature determines sex — above 30°C produces females, below 30°C produces males. Hatching at 50–70 days. Adult males have longer tails and front flipper claws.
funFact: Named for the green colour of their body fat — caused by their algae-heavy diet — not for their shell colour.

name: Lagoon Damselfish
diet: Algae
behavior: Non-migratory. Lives around branching coral. Males guard and aerate eggs, and create a stage for mating display.
reproduction: Oviparous. Distinct pairs during breeding. Eggs are demersal and adhere to substrate. Males guard and aerate eggs.
funFact: Actively tends its own algal garden — weeding out undesirable species and guarding it against other herbivores like a tiny underwater farmer.

name: Flathead Grey Mullet
diet: Detritus, algae and benthic organisms
behavior: Found in schools over sand. Sexually mature after 3–4 years. Euryhaline — can adapt to different salinities of water.
reproduction: Females spawn 0.8–2.6 million eggs which develop at sea.
funFact: Euryhaline — can thrive in fresh, brackish, or salt water, making it one of the most adaptable fish in the ocean.

name: Blue and Gold Fusilier
diet: Zooplankton
behavior: Adults found in schools in lagoons and reefs. Mass spawning on lunar cycles.
reproduction: Oviparous. Small pelagic eggs. Mating has six stages: nuzzling, males joining courtship, spiraling to surface, pair spawning, sperm released by sneakers, post-spawning.
funFact: Has an elaborate six-stage mating ritual — including 'sneaker' males who release sperm during another pair's spawning.

name: Crocodile Needlefish
diet: Small fish and benthic crustaceans
behavior: Pelagic. Solitary. Largest in its family. Teeth point anteriorly when juvenile.
reproduction: Oviparous. Eggs attach to objects in the water by tendrils on the egg's surface. Mature at 50cm (males), 55cm (females).
funFact: The largest needlefish species — at 1.5 metres, they can be dangerous to night divers as they're attracted to lights and can leap from the water at speed.

name: Bar Tailed Goatfish
diet: Primarily small crustaceans
behavior: Euryhaline. Benthopelagic. Often in groups.
funFact: Uses its chin barbels like metal detectors — sweeping them across the sand to sense hidden prey buried beneath.

name: Indian Goatfish
diet: Benthic invertebrates — crabs, amphipods, shrimps, small octopus, polychaete worms and small fish
behavior: Inhabit sandy and silty bottoms. Occur solitary or in schools.
funFact: Has an impressive menu — eats crabs, amphipods, shrimps, octopus, worms and small fish, all detected with sensitive chin barbels.

name: Virgate Rabbitfish
diet: Benthic seaweeds and zooplankton
behavior: Larger juveniles and adults occur in pairs. Juveniles move to reefs in small groups. Venomous. Camouflages at night by going darker (crypsis).
reproduction: Oviparous.
funFact: Changes colour at night — going much darker as camouflage (called crypsis). The same colour change happens when they're stressed.

name: Gold Saddle Rabbitfish
diet: Benthic algae
behavior: In schools throughout life. Active at night. Venomous. Can tolerate a wide range of water salinities.
funFact: Unlike most rabbitfish that pair off as adults, these stay in schools their entire lives — safety in numbers at all ages.

name: Orange Spined Unicornfish
diet: Mainly leafy brown algae but also zooplankton
behavior: Found in coral reefs. Benthopelagic. Adults found in small groups. Pair spawning. Venomous.
reproduction: Pair spawning.
funFact: Named for the bright orange scalpel-like spines on its tail — used as self-defence weapons that can inflict serious cuts.

name: Sergeant Major Fish
diet: Algae, small crustaceans, small fish and various invertebrate larvae
behavior: Juveniles common in tide pools. Adults common over shallow reef tops. Form large feeding aggregations. Males create nests on rock.
reproduction: Oviparous. Distinct pairing during breeding. Eggs are demersal and stick to substrate. Males guard and aerate eggs. Adult males turn bluish when guarding eggs.
funFact: Males turn a striking bluish colour when guarding their eggs — and they also clean green turtles at cleaning stations.

name: Soldierfish
diet: Mainly plankton
behavior: Inhabits caves and hides under ledges and Acropora coral. Benthopelagic.
funFact: Has enormous eyes relative to body size — an adaptation for feeding on plankton in the dim light of caves and overhangs.

name: Blue Spotted Grouper
diet: Mainly fishes (75–95%) and sometimes crustaceans
behavior: Non-migratory. Common. Mature adults found in groups of up to 12. May follow and cooperate hunting with other predators such as octopus and eel.
funFact: Hunts cooperatively with octopus and eels — a rare example of interspecies teamwork in the marine world.

name: Squirrelfish
diet: Isopods, polychaetes, small crabs and other crustaceans
behavior: Common species inhabiting shallow coral reefs. Benthopelagic. Occurs in ledges and crevices during the day. Tends to occur in groups. Has a venomous spine on its preopercle.
funFact: Has a venomous spine on its cheek (preopercle) — an unexpected defence for such a small, seemingly harmless fish.

name: Longfin Batfish
diet: Omnivore — algae, plankton and small invertebrates
behavior: Large adults live in sheltered bays and on deeper sites offshore. Sometimes found in large schools. Social fish.
funFact: One of the most curious and social fish on the reef — batfish will often approach and follow divers for entire dives.

name: Scribbled Filefish
diet: Algae, seagrass, hydrozoans, gorgonians, colonial anemones and tunicates
behavior: Inhabit lagoons and coral reefs. Benthopelagic. Closely related to triggerfish — also has a trigger spine.
funFact: At 110cm, it's the largest filefish species — closely related to triggerfish and shares their characteristic trigger spine.

name: Chevron Barracuda
diet: Teleosts (including juvenile jacks and scad), molluscs and crustaceans
behavior: Nocturnally active but found in large groups during the day for protection. Tornado swimming to confuse predators. Attracted to shiny objects.
reproduction: High fecundity rate.
funFact: Forms spectacular 'barracuda tornadoes' during the day — swirling in tight formations to confuse predators through safety in numbers.

name: Yellowtail Barracuda
diet: Small fish and squid
behavior: Found in schools in sheltered, tropical reefs for protection. Safety in numbers, and tornado swimming to confuse predators. Attracted to shiny objects.
funFact: Attracted to shiny, reflective objects — divers are advised to remove jewellery before entering barracuda territory.

name: Pickhandle Barracuda
diet: Mainly fishes but also sometimes squid
behavior: Solitary or small groups as adults, juveniles form larger groups. Migratory. Nocturnal hunters.
reproduction: Dioecism — external fertilisation.
funFact: Named for the dark bar markings on their sides resembling pickaxe handles — at 1.5 metres, the largest commonly schooling barracuda.

name: Great Barracuda
diet: Fishes, cephalopods and sometimes shrimps
behavior: Solitary. Ambush predator — can reach speeds of up to 27mph. Juveniles found in sheltered reefs. Maturity at 66cm.
reproduction: Females can release about 5,000–30,000 eggs.
funFact: Can accelerate to 27mph in a burst — faster than most sports cars relative to body length, making it one of the ocean's fastest ambush predators.

name: Yellow Striped Scad
diet: Ostracods, gastropods, euphausiids and crustaceans
behavior: Amphidromous. Form large demersal schools. Fecundity is directly related to the size of the fish.
reproduction: Pelagic spawners. Fecundity is directly related to body size.
funFact: Juveniles have been known to shelter inside large jellyfish — using them as a protective, stinging home.

name: Golden Trevally
diet: Crustaceans, invertebrates and small fishes
behavior: Found either solitary or in small groups. Found in deep lagoons and reefs.
funFact: Juveniles display 'piloting behaviour' — swimming alongside sharks, rays and other large fish for protection from predators.

name: Big Eye Trevally
diet: Fish, squid and crustaceans
behavior: Pelagic. Often seen schooling in the day. Nocturnal hunters.
funFact: Schools together during the day for safety, then splits up at night to become solitary nocturnal hunters — living a double life.

name: Whale Shark
diet: Planktonic and nektonic prey — filter feeders that feed by suction gulping
behavior: Largest known extant fish species, up to 18m. Pelagic. Commonly found individually. Associated with groups of pelagic fish. Highly migratory but return to same sites annually. Skin up to 15cm thick.
reproduction: Ovoviviparous. Litter size of over 300 pups (40–60cm at birth). Distinct mating pairs with embrace. Sexually mature after 8–9 years.
funFact: Each whale shark has a unique pattern of spots — like a fingerprint — used by scientists to identify and track individuals worldwide.

name: Remora
diet: Small fish, bits of host's prey and parasites on host
behavior: Most abundant remora in warm waters. Attaches temporarily to hosts including whale sharks and turtles.
funFact: Has a modified dorsal fin that acts as a suction cup — hitching rides on whale sharks, turtles and even boats for free transport.

name: Rainbow Runner
diet: Invertebrates, larger crustaceans and small fish
behavior: Normally spotted near the surface. May form large schools. Highly migratory.
reproduction: Pelagic spawners. Oviparous.
funFact: One of the most colourful open-water fish — iridescent rainbow stripes along their body make them unmistakable.

name: Cobia
diet: Crabs, fishes and squid
behavior: Solitary or in small groups. Pelagic. Follows larger animals to scavenge.
reproduction: Pelagic spawners.
funFact: Its closest living relative is the shark sucker (remora) — despite looking nothing alike, they share a common ancestor.

name: Blacktip Reef Shark
diet: Fishes, crustaceans, cephalopods and other molluscs
behavior: Females larger than males. Inhabits coral reefs, intertidal zone, near reef drop-offs. Occurs singularly or in small groups.
reproduction: Viviparous. 2–4 pups per litter. 8–9 months gestation. Pups 33–52cm at birth. Distinct pairing with embrace. Maturity at 91–120cm.
funFact: One of the few shark species commonly seen in very shallow water — sometimes in water barely deep enough to cover their dorsal fin.

name: Talang Queenfish
diet: Fishes, cephalopods, small invertebrates and other pelagic prey
behavior: Usually found in small groups.
funFact: A prized game fish that puts up a spectacular fight — known for acrobatic leaps when hooked.
